On the night of Ramchandra Ray's escape, Udayaditya is the one in control, commanding Sitaram to open the door, threatening him with a sword, and binding him from head to foot. This dynamic reverses on the night of Udayaditya's own escape, where Sitaram becomes the rescuer who grabs Udayaditya to carry him out of the burning prison and protects him by stepping between him and Rukmini's attack.

Pratapaditya's initial, failed plan was to have two Pathan men kill Basanta Ray when he took shelter at the inn in Simultali on his way to Jessore. The provided context does not mention the method that finally succeeds in ending the old king's life.
The provided context does not contain information regarding the locations, disguises, or physical retaliation from Rammohan Mal.
Pratapaditya threatens to have Udayaditya imprisoned if Surma does not leave.

Vibha's refusal led King Ramchandra Ray to feel insulted, which eventually prompted him to marry another woman. Consequently, when Vibha later arrived at the palace, the King rejected her, claimed he did not know her, and called her a beggar woman, leading Vibha to spend the rest of her life in Kashi.
The provided text does not mention a signet ring or how it was used as evidence to justify Udayaditya's imprisonment.

The escape led Basanta Ray to bribe the guard Bhagavat with money to protect Udayaditya by having him lie about who tied him up; when word of these "secret stipends" reached Pratapaditya, he ordered Surma to be exiled to her paternal home.

The song signifies emptiness and loneliness, reflecting the sorrow of losing loved ones and the feeling that there is no longer any joy in singing when those the singer longs to embrace are gone. Basanta Ray sings it in two contrasting contexts: first, in solitary reflection while gazing at the setting sun from the veranda of his palace in Raigarh, and second, while holding Vibha's hand after discovering that Surma's room in Jessore is silent, dark, and empty.

Rukmini's failure to stop Udayaditya's midnight escape allowed him and Basanta Ray to reach Raigarh. Later, Rukmini located Udayaditya in Raigarh and alerted the soldiers, which enabled Muktiyar Khan to arrive with and execute Pratapaditya's order for the death sentence of Basanta Ray.

Pratapaditya initially received Ramchandra Ray with indifference and a lack of royal fanfare, providing a minimal welcome party, a small elephant, and dismissing him in his royal chamber by interrupting him and calling him "my boy." Later, in Ramchandra's own court, Pratapaditya's status was addressed with extreme contempt and ridicule; courtiers claimed his father had to beg for royal anointment from Ramchandra's grandfather, Ramai Bhand compared Pratapaditya's ancestors to earthworms and leeches, and the minister asserted that Pratapaditya's family was so inferior that marrying into Ramchandra's family redeemed seven generations of Pratapaditya's lineage.

Rukmini re-enters Udayaditya's life by visiting him in his bedchamber during a storm, pretending to be a beggar and manipulating him into giving her a ring. Later, while Udayaditya is in Raigarh planning to escape, she points him out to soldiers, shouting, "Here! Here is your Yubaraj—right here!" This leads to Udayaditya's arrest and the subsequent execution of the King of Raigarh, Basanta Ray, by order of the Maharaja.

Udayaditya's early lament stems from his belief that he lacks the intellect for ruling, noting that his father viewed him as an examiner rather than with affection and that his attempt to manage the Hosenkhali pargana resulted in disorder and decreased revenue. He felt he was born only as an heir rather than a son and was considered a fool and a disgrace to the family. This culminates in a formal renunciation when Udayaditya, brought back to Jessore as a prisoner, agrees with his father that he is not worthy of the kingdom. He formally renounces the throne by swearing an oath before Ma-Kali that he will never accept any part of the kingdom, sit on the throne, or touch the royal scepter, naming Samaraditya as the heir.

Ramchandra Ray's letter, which stated that he had renounced Vibha and she should not be sent to Chandradwip again, was intercepted by Nayanchand, who, fearing Pratapaditya's reaction, gave it to the Mahishi instead of delivering it to the Maharaj. It was subsequently misrepresented when Basanta Ray, after reading the letter, advised the Mahishi to announce to the household that Ramchandra had written a letter requesting that Vibha be sent to Chandradwip.

Pratapaditya attempted to trap Ramchandra Ray by ordering guards to block the canal adjoining the royal palace with large sal wood logs. This failed because Rammohan and his followers pulled the boat up and over the logs. Furthermore, the guards' pursuit was delayed because they could not find a flintlock for their gun, and the man sent to fetch a pursuit boat stopped at Harimudi’s shop to smoke tobacco and demand a debt payment from Ramashankar.

Basanta Ray's relationship with his sitar mirrors his emotional state regarding Udayaditya: when forced to leave him, Basanta Ray plays the instrument "furiously with a great clatter" before eventually putting it down and declaring that he is leaving it behind and will not play it anymore. Later, when Khan Saheb asks about the instrument, Basanta Ray reveals that while he still has the sitar, its strings have broken and it can no longer be tuned. However, upon Udayaditya's return to Raigarh, Basanta Ray resumes playing the sitar in an effort to make Udayaditya happy.

Earlier, Udayaditya was known to mix with common people and oppose his father by taking their side; he would have stopped a royal official from beating a subject to protect them without considering the consequences. After Surma's death, his former courage vanished, and he could no longer fight against oppression with all his might. When he saw a royal official beating a subject, he ran into his house because he could not bear to see the suffering, and he no longer had the courage to help oppressed individuals like Bhagavat and Sitaram with money, either openly or secretly.

Sitaram undoes the damage by orchestrating Udayaditya's escape. He coordinates a palace fire and has his men set fire to Udayaditya's empty prison cell, into which he throws bones, a skull, and Udayaditya's sword to make it appear as though the Yubaraj died in the blaze.

On the first occasion, Rammohan Mal extracted Ramchandra Ray by tying thick, large sheets together to create a huge rope, which he secured to a pillar on the palace roof. He lifted the King onto his back, climbed down the rope, and then gripped the rope with his teeth to carefully lower the King into a boat before jumping down himself. On the second occasion, he attempted to extract the Thakurani (Vibha), but he returned alone because she did not wish to come.

In Jessore, Vibha is heavily adorned by the Mahishi with eight thick gold bangles and one huge diamond bracelet on each arm, a large nose ring, and a specific coiffure. In contrast, when she seeks out Ramchandra Ray in Chandradwip, she refuses a palanquin to go as a "common subject" or "beggar-woman," dresses as a common woman, enters the palace like a maidservant, and wears a veil.

In his bedchamber, the window symbolizes intimacy and companionship, serving as the place where Udayaditya and Surma would sit together in the evening. In the prison cell, the window symbolizes hope and liberation; by looking at the sky and birds through it, Udayaditya feels naturally free and believes that his "cage" will one day break.

The dynamic begins as a flirtatious financial arrangement where Sitaram, attracted to Rukmini's beauty and wealth, borrows money from her. Their relationship is characterized by playful romantic banter, with Sitaram using terms of endearment such as "Pranpriye" and "Priyatame."

The relationship devolves when Udayaditya is imprisoned. Sitaram visits Rukmini to curse her, threaten to set her house on fire, and vow to grind her face into the stone floor and smear it with lime and soot. Rukmini responds with intense hatred, viewing Sitaram as a wretched fool and claiming the Yubaraj as her own.

The encounter becomes life-threatening after Sitaram helps orchestrate the Yubaraj's escape and fakes his death. Believing Rukmini is dead, Sitaram enters her home to steal her money. When he finds her alive, he attempts to feign affection to make up with her, which only further enrages her. Rukmini declares that she has returned from the door of Yama to lay Sitaram and the Yubaraj in an oven and smear her body with their ashes. She pursues him with a curved blade, striking the floor where he had stood, forcing Sitaram to flee Jessore.

Sitaram's strategy begins by creating chaos through orchestrated fires in five or six rooms and using loyal commoners and servants to sabotage firefighting efforts by breaking water pots and pouring water where there was no fire. Amidst this distraction, Sitaram rescues Udayaditya from his prison cell. The strategy evolves into a cover-up when Sitaram's men set fire to the now-empty prison cell, and Sitaram throws a skull, bones, and Udayaditya's sword into the flames. He then completes the deception by falsely claiming the roof of the cell had collapsed and that Udayaditya was unresponsive, leading the guards and officials to believe the Yubaraj had died in the fire.

Ramchandra Ray was not grateful and felt that Udayaditya saving his life was bound to happen and that it would have been wrong if it had not. He believed that everyone would naturally unite to save him if he were in peril and interpreted Udayaditya's actions as being done for the sake of his own sister rather than saving Ramchandra Ray's life being the main objective.
The provided text does not contain information regarding the Emperor of Delhi being used as a mechanism to imprison Pratapaditya's son.

The grooming of Vibha's hair marks Surma's presence through acts of comfort and personal preference; Surma gently runs her fingers through Vibha's hair when she is distressed and styles it to Vibha's liking when the Mahishi's hairstyle is unbearable. Surma's tragic absence is marked when Vibha sits quietly waiting for Surma to braid her hair, but it remains unbraided because Surma is gone.
